evidence.excerpt: Here’s something: my chatGPT quietly assumed I had ADHD for around 9 months, up until October 2025. I don’t suffer from ADHD. I only found out through an answer that began “As you have ADHD..” I had it stop right there, and asked it to tell me exactly where it got this information; the date, the title of the chat, the exact moment it took this data on as an attribute of mine. It was unable to specify any of it, aside from nine months previous. It continued to insist I had ADHD, and that I told it I did, but was unable to reference exactly when/where. I asked “do you think it’s dangerous that you have assumed I have a medical / neurological condition for this long? What if you gave me incorrect advice based on this assumption?” to which it answered a paraphrased mea culpa, offered to forget the attribute, and moved the conversation on. This is a class action waiting to happen.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says ChatGPT falsely assumed a medical condition, could have given incorrect advice, and calls it 'a class action waiting to happen,' which is strong criticism of its safety and trustworthiness.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT figured out in 5 minutes the reason behind a pretty serious chronic problem that her very good doctors hadn't been able to figure out in 3 years. Her doctors came around to the possibility, tested out the hypothesis, and it was 100% right. She's doing great now

annotations.rationale: The commenter credits ChatGPT with correctly identifying a serious chronic problem that doctors missed for years and says it was '100% right,' which is strong praise for usefulness and quality.

evidence.excerpt: I don't get the Gemini 3 hype... yes it's their first usable model, but its not even close to what Opus 4.5 and GPT 5.2 can do. Maybe on Benchmarks... but I'm forced to use Gemini at work everyday, while I use Opus 4.5 / GPT 5.2 privately every day... and Gemini is just lacking so much wit, creativity and multi-step problem solving skills compared to Opus. Not to mention that Gemini CLI is a pain to use - after getting used to the smoothness of Claude Code.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Gemini 3 is only Google's 'first usable model,' is 'not even close' to rivals, lacks wit and problem-solving skill, and that Gemini CLI is 'a pain to use'.
The commenter says Opus 4.5 outperforms Gemini and praises the 'smoothness of Claude Code,' indicating a favorable comparison for Anthropic.

evidence.excerpt: I've tried this consistently since GPT 3.5 came out, with every single SOTA model up to GPT 5.1 Codex Max and Opus 4.5. Every single time, I get something that works, yes, but then when I start self-reviewing the code, preparing to submit it to coworkers, I end up rewriting about 70% of the thing. So many important details are subpar about the AI solution, and many times fundamental architectural issues cripple any attempt at prompting my way out of it

evidence.excerpt: If nothing else, this was likely driven by Google being the most stable of the AI labs. Gemini is objectively a good model (whether it's #1 or #5 in ranking aside) so Apple can confidently deliver a good (enough) product. Also for Apple, they know their provider has ridiculously deep pockets, a good understanding and infrastructure in place for large enterprises, and a fairly diversified revenue stream. Going with Anthropic or OpenAI, despite on the surface having that clean Apple smell and feel, carries a lot of risk Apple's part. Both companies are far underwater, liable to take risks, and liable to drown if they even fall a bit behind.

evidence.excerpt: A bit unrelated, but if you ever find a malicious use of Anthropic APIs like that, you can just upload the key to a GitHub Gist or a public repo - Anthropic is a GitHub scanning partner, so the key will be revoked almost instantly (you can delete the gist afterwards). It works for a lot of other providers too, including OpenAI (which also has file APIs, by the way).

annotations.rationale: The commenter praises Anthropic's rapid secret-revocation setup via GitHub scanning as a useful security feature.
OpenAI is mentioned as also benefiting from the same quick key-revocation behavior, which is favorable security evidence.

evidence.excerpt: Unlike /slash commands, skills attempt to be magical. A skill is just "Here's how you can extract files: {instructions}". Claude then has to decide when you're trying to invoke a skill. So perhaps any time you say "decompress" or "extract" in the context of files, it will use the instructions from that skill. It seems like this + no skill "registration" makes it much easier for prompt injection to sneak new abilities into the token stream and then make it so you never know if you might trigger one with normal prompting.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly criticizes Claude's skill design as making prompt injection much easier and unpredictable.

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment is mostly neutral overall, with OpenAI showing mixed reactions from both praise and criticism [E2][E3][E4][E5], while Anthropic, Google Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot sit in a close tie with limited evidence and mostly mixed or negative comments [E1][E3][E4][E6].

ranking.summary: Google Gemini appears neutral overall but with limited evidence, and the main comment is negative about its reluctance to search the web; it is also part of a close tie with Anthropic and Microsoft Copilot [E3].
Microsoft Copilot is neutral overall with limited evidence, based on a negative coding-assistant anecdote, and it is in a close tie with Anthropic and Google Gemini [E6].

Anthropic lands in mixed neutral territory: one comment criticizes Claude’s reliability, while another says Claude has better UI and answers, and it is in a close tie with Google Gemini and Microsoft Copilot [E1][E4].
OpenAI is mixed neutral but draws the most discussion, with comments praising its research and guidance while others criticize silent downgrades and weaker instruction following [E2][E3][E4][E5].

evidence.excerpt: I am using API mode, and it's clear that there are times when the Claude model just gives up. And it is very noticeable because the model just does the most dumb things possible. "You have a bug in line 23." "Oh yes, this solution is bugged, let me delete the whole feature." That one-line fix I could make even with ChatGPT 3.5 can't just happen. Workflows that I use and are very reproducible start to flake and then fail. After a certain number of tokens per day, it becomes unusable. I like Claude, but I don't understand why they would do this.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Claude and strongly criticizes its reliability and usefulness, calling it unusable after a token threshold.

evidence.excerpt: It's even worse with ChatGPT and the like. E.g. I had to learn the hard way that at >45k input tokens ChatGPT 5.2 Thinking Extended bumps its intelligence down so hard that it can't follow basic instructions (or it somehow truncates the input, losing the instructions). It sucks to lose confidence in an otherwise great tool. I would 100x prefer being forced to back-off, or getting a straight-no, than getting silently downgraded. Transparency is a big deal.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly criticizes ChatGPT for silently degrading capability on long inputs and frames this as a transparency and trust problem.

evidence.excerpt: Also, it seems like Gemini is really averse to googling (which is ironic by itself) and ChatGPT, at least in the Thinking modes loves to look up current and correct info. If I ask something a bit more involved in Extended Thinking mode, it will think for several minutes and look up more than 100 sources. It's really good, practically a Deep Research inside of a normal chat.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Gemini is 'really averse to googling,' a negative comparison against ChatGPT's search behavior.
The commenter explicitly praises ChatGPT Thinking modes for looking up many sources and calls the result 'really good' and like Deep Research.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT 5.2 has been a good motivator for me to try out other LLMs because of how bad it is. Both 5.1 and 5.2 have been downgrades in terms of instruction following and accuracy, but 5.2 especially so. The upside is that that's had me using Claude much more, and I like a lot of things about it, both in terms of UI and the answers.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly calls ChatGPT 5.2 'bad' and says recent versions are downgrades in instruction following and accuracy.
The commenter explicitly says they are using Claude more and like many things about it, including the UI and answers.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT took the time to ask me questions and based on my answers, narrowed down a particularly scary diagnosis and gave excellent instructions on how to get to a local hospital in a foreign country, what to ask for, and that I didn't have to worry very much because it sounded very typical for what I had. The level of reassurance that I was doing everything right actually made me feel less scared, because it was a pretty serious problem. Everything it told me was 100% correct and it guided me perfectly.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits ChatGPT with accurate guidance, excellent instructions, and perfect guidance in a medical situation.

evidence.excerpt: Today I asked copilot agent a question about a selector in a cypress test and it requested to run a python command in my terminal.

annotations.rationale: In coding-assistant context, 'copilot agent' most likely refers to Microsoft/GitHub Copilot, and the commenter describes an obviously unhelpful suggestion.

If you want to run this full time to organise your mailbox and your agenda, it's probably cheaper to hire a real human personal assistant.

evidence.excerpt: It was significantly less focused and reliable than Opus 4.5 at implementing some mostly-fleshed-out specs I had lying around for some needed modifications to an enterprise TS node/express service. I was a bit disappointed tbh, the speed via fireworks.ai is great, they're doing great work on the hosting side. But I found the model had to double-back to fix type issues, broken tests, etc, far more than Opus 4.5 which churned through the tasks with almost zero errors. In fact, I gave the resulting code to Opus, simply said it looked "sloppy" and Opus cleaned it up very quickly.

annotations.rationale: Opus 4.5 is described as more focused and reliable, with almost zero errors and fast cleanup of sloppy code.

The commenter uses 'Gemini' style as a mildly unfavorable comparison, implying bland or less distinctive output.

I then went back to add a test by hand, and after looking more closely at the output there were like 50 wtf worthy things scattered in there. Sure they ran, but it was bad in all sorts of ways. And this was just writing something very basic.

evidence.excerpt: One of the more annoying software that does this is the copilot Office 365 on the web. Every time (!) I open it, it shows a popup on how to add files to the context. That itself would be annoying, but it also steals focus!

days.headlineSummary: Sentiment was most favorable toward Anthropic and Google Gemini, mixed on OpenAI, and negative on Microsoft Copilot, with limited evidence behind Copilot and Gemini [E1][E2][E3].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot was negative, driven by a comment describing Microsoft and GitHub as pushing Copilot on users for years [E2].

Google Gemini had a positive but limited-evidence signal from a commenter who said Gemini 3 flash seemed smarter, faster to iterate with, and had better common sense [E1].
Anthropic drew positive reactions: one commenter said Claude Code handled the same coding tasks quickly and successfully, and another credited Anthropic with building a tool that took off [E1][E2].

evidence.excerpt: I would rather YOLO code with Gemini 3 flash, since it's actually smarter in my assessment, and at least I can iterate faster, and it feels like it has better common sense. Just as an example, I found an old, terrible app I made years ago for our firm that handles room reservations. I told it to update from Bootstrap to Flowbite UI. Codex just took forever to make a mess, installed version 2.7 when 4.0.1 is the latest, even when I explicitly stated that it should use the absolute latest version. Then it tried to install it and failed, so it reverted to the outdated CDN. I gave the same task to Claude Code. Same prompt. It one-shotted it quickly. Then I asked it to swap out ALL the fetch logic to have SPA-like functionality with the new beta 4 version of HTMX, and it one-shot that too in the time Codex spent just trying to read a few files in the project. This reminds me of the feeling I had when I got the Nokia N800. It was so promising on paper, but the product was so bad and terrible to use that I knew Nokia was done for. If this was their take on what an acceptable smartphone could be, it proves that the whole foundation is doomed. If this is OpenAI's take on what an agentic coding assistant should be—something that can run by itself and iterate until it completes its task in an intelligent and creative way.... OpenAI is doomed.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Gemini 3 flash is smarter, faster to iterate with, and has better common sense than Codex.
The commenter says Claude Code completed the same tasks quickly and successfully in one shot, strongly favoring it on coding performance.
The commenter describes Codex as slow and messy and concludes with 'OpenAI is doomed,' making this strong negative evidence about OpenAI's coding assistant quality.

evidence.excerpt: It really says something that MS/Github has been trying to shovel Copilot down our throats for years, and Anthropic just builds a tool in a short period of time and it takes off.

annotations.rationale: The commenter characterizes Microsoft's promotion of Copilot as 'trying to shovel Copilot down our throats,' a strongly negative comparison.
The commenter credits Anthropic with building a tool that 'takes off,' expressing approval relative to Copilot.

evidence.excerpt: Genuinely excited to try this out. I've started using Codex much more heavily in the past two months and honestly, it's been shockingly good. Not perfect mind you, but it keeps impressing me with what it's able to "get". It often gets stuff wrong, and at times runs with faulty assumptions, but overall it's no worse than having average L3-L4 engs at your disposal.

annotations.rationale: In the Codex App story on openai.com, the commenter says Codex has been 'shockingly good' and keeps impressing them, despite some caveats.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew the most positive discussion, Microsoft Copilot drew the most negative, and OpenAI was mildly negative; Anthropic’s praise outweighed a small openness complaint, while Copilot was criticized for hurting code quality [E1][E2][E4][E5][E6].

evidence.excerpt: If you are building a product today, the feature you are working on _is not done_ until Claude Code can use it. A skill and an MCP isn't a "nice to have", it is going to be as important as SEO and accessibility, with extremely similar work to do to enable it.

Framing MCP support as avoiding being 'locked into ... Codex' presents Codex as a lock-in option, a mild negative toward OpenAI's ecosystem openness.

evidence.excerpt: I’ve been using Gemini 3 Pro on a historical document archiving project for an old club. Anyways, I was getting decent results on a first pass with 50 page chunks but ended up doing 1 page at a time (accuracy probably 95%). For each page, I submit the page for a transcription pass followed by a translation of the returned transcription. About 2370 pages and sitting at about $50 in Gemini API billing. The output will need manual review, but the time savings is impressive.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew the sharpest criticism, Google Gemini was also viewed negatively, OpenAI landed mixed to neutral, and Microsoft Copilot had limited evidence with a small positive note [E5][E10][E1][E6].

ranking.summary: Anthropic was discussed mostly negatively, with complaints about reliability, closed policies, privacy concerns, and leadership messaging, though one comparison favored Claude over Gemini for practical development work [E5][E6][E7][E10][E11][E1].

Google Gemini was viewed negatively overall, with criticism of frustrating development behavior, confusing billing and access, unstable preview/release status, and troubling Arabic safety behavior, despite one strong value-for-money defense [E1][E3][E4][E8][E2].

OpenAI came through as mixed to neutral: some commenters praised Codex and open policy choices, while others criticized ChatGPT translation quality and CEO statements about reducing software engineering demand [E5][E6][E9][E11].
Microsoft Copilot had limited evidence, but the available comment was positive about its openness and OpenCode compatibility [E6].

evidence.excerpt: Gemini is consistently the most frustrating model I've used for development. It's stunningly good at reasoning, design, and generating the raw code, but it just falls over a lot when actually trying to get things done, especially compared to Claude Opus.

I can accept specific model limits, not an up/down in terms of reliability. And don't even let me get started on how bad Claude client has become. Others are finally catching up and gpt-5.3-codex is definitely better than opus-4.6

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic draws mixed-but-slightly-positive discussion from praise for productivity and out-of-the-box behavior, while OpenAI skews negative over trust and access complaints [E1][E4][E5][E7][E2][E3][E6].

Anthropic is discussed more positively than negatively, with comments praising Claude’s capability and productivity, though one thread criticizes its login experience and another flags transparency concerns [E1][E4][E5][E7][E2].

evidence.excerpt: The article is forgetting about Anthropic which currently has the best agentic programmer and was the backbone for the recent OpenClaw assistants.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Anthropic and says it has 'the best agentic programmer,' a strong endorsement of capability.

evidence.excerpt: We ran queries across ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity asking for product recommendations in ~30 B2B categories. The overlap between what each model recommends is surprisingly low -- around 40% agreement on the top 5 picks for any given category. And the correlation with Google search rankings? About 0.08. So we already have a world where which CRM or analytics tool gets recommended depends on which model someone happens to ask, and nobody -- not the models, not the brands, not the users -- has any transparency into why. That's arguably more dangerous than explicit ads, because at least with ads you know you're being sold to.

annotations.rationale: ChatGPT is explicitly named, and the excerpt criticizes opaque recommendations as lacking transparency and being 'more dangerous than explicit ads.'
Claude is explicitly named, and the same passage criticizes opaque recommendations as lacking transparency and being 'more dangerous than explicit ads.'

evidence.excerpt: Sure, it's still better than also sending everything up to OpenAI, but that doesn’t make it harmless or less creepy.

annotations.rationale: The explicit OpenAI mention is framed as privacy-invasive and 'creepy,' indicating negative sentiment.

evidence.excerpt: Not exactly a surprise Claude did this out of the box with minimal prompting

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Claude and strongly criticizes its login flow and fundamentals as 'screwed up.'

They also seems to be doing well w.r.t. competition.

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment skews negative on Google Gemini בגלל complaints about draconian enforcement, lockouts, and fake positives, while OpenAI gets the warmest notes and Anthropic lands mixed with both praise and criticism [E3][E4][E6][E5].

ranking.summary: Google Gemini is the most negative of the group, with commenters calling its enforcement draconian, criticizing warning-free account locks and billing, and saying it produces the most fake positives and overly rosy answers [E3][E4][E6].

Anthropic comes across as mixed: Claude Code is called brilliant for lecture-prep workflows, but other comments note token stalls and a higher false positive rate alongside its stronger detection ability [E1][E2][E5].
OpenAI draws the most positive comments, with GPT described as impressive for a consistent 0% false positive rate and Codex praised for faster, more reliable PRs than Claude Code [E5][E2].

evidence.excerpt: I use Claude Code for lecture prep. I craft a detailed and ordered set of lecture notes in a Quarto file and then have a dedicated claude code skill for translating those notes into Slidev slides, in the style that I like. Once that's done, much like the author, I go through the slides and make commented annotations like "this should be broken into two slides" or "this should be a side-by-side" or "use your generate clipart skill to throw an image here alongside these bullets" and "pull in the code example from ../examples/foo." It works brilliantly.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Claude Code and strongly endorses its performance with 'It works brilliantly.'

evidence.excerpt: And I'm only on the $20 plans for Codex/Gemini vs. spending $100/month on CC for half year prior and move quicker w/ no stall outs due to token consumption, which was regularly happening w/ CC by the 5th day. Codex rarely dips below 70% available context when it puts up a PR after an execution run. Roughly 4/5 PRs are without issue, which is flipped against what I experienced with CC and only using planning mode.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly praises Codex as quicker and more reliable in context usage and PR outcomes than CC.
Gemini is grouped with Codex on cheaper plans and contrasted favorably against CC for speed and lack of token-related stalls.
In the Claude Code story context, 'CC' refers to Claude Code; the user reports frequent token-consumption stall outs and worse outcomes.

evidence.excerpt: This is draconian. > Our investigation specifically confirmed that the use of your credentials within the third-party tool “open claw” for testing purposes constitutes a violation of the Google Terms of Service [1]. This is due to the use of Antigravity servers to power a non-Antigravity product. I must be transparent and inform you that, in accordance with Google’s policy, this situation falls under a zero tolerance policy, and we are unable to reverse the suspension. I am truly sorry to share this difficult news with you.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly condemns Google's AI-service enforcement as 'draconian' and highlights irreversible zero-tolerance suspension.

evidence.excerpt: Of course Google can restrict how their API is accessed. But locking paid accounts with no warning, no explanation email, and no functioning support path while continuing to charge $249/month is a different problem entirely. A reasonable enforcement process would have been a warning email, grace period to stop using the tool, then restriction. What an awful way to lose trust, locking out their users but billing them all the same.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt strongly criticizes the service for no-warning lockouts, broken support, continued billing, and loss of trust.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew clearly positive reactions for Claude/Sonnet helping with coding and reasoning, while OpenAI’s feedback was mixed with one praise and one criticism [E1][E2][E4][E5].

ranking.summary: OpenAI landed in mixed neutral territory: one comment praised Codex in a successful porting workflow, while another criticized ChatGPT for missing a simple question [E1][E5].

Anthropic was viewed positively overall, with several comments praising Claude/Sonnet for fast code fixes, successful porting, and strong reasoning, though one note criticized Claude Code account bans [E1][E2][E3][E4].

evidence.excerpt: I used Claude Code and Codex for the translation. This was human-directed, not autonomous code generation. I decided what to port, in what order, and what the Rust code should look like. It was hundreds of small prompts, steering the agents where things needed to go. After the initial translation, I ran multiple passes of adversarial review, asking different models to analyze the code for mistakes and bad patterns. > The requirement from the start was byte-for-byte identical output from both pipelines. The result was about 25,000 lines of Rust, and the entire port took about two weeks. The same work would have taken me multiple months to do by hand. We’ve verified that every AST produced by the Rust parser is identical to the C++ one, and all bytecode generated by the Rust compiler is identical to the C++ compiler’s output. Zero regressions across the board This is the way. Coding assistants are also really great at porting from one language to the other, especially if you have existing tests.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Claude Code and presents it as part of a successful port that saved months of work, then broadly endorses coding assistants for this task.
The comment explicitly names Codex and describes the translation as successful and much faster than manual work, followed by praise for coding assistants at porting.

evidence.excerpt: I put Sonnet 4.6 on the case, and it wrote a small patch, compiled and installed it in a matter of minutes, without giving it any instructions other than to look at errors and apply a fix. I definitely would have just given up without the AI.

annotations.rationale: Sonnet 4.6 is an Anthropic model family name, and the commenter strongly praises its usefulness by saying it solved the problem in minutes and they would have given up without it.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Code is banning accounts for usage of pi (or rather, how pi is configured to use Claude models)

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly attributes account bans to Claude Code usage, expressing a clear complaint about access restrictions.

evidence.excerpt: To sonnet 4.6 if you tell it first that "You're being tested for intelligence." It answers correctly 100% of the times.

annotations.rationale: The commenter reports Sonnet 4.6 gets the benchmark right every time under a simple prompt change, which is direct praise of its reasoning performance.

evidence.excerpt: FWIW though, asking ChatGPT "My car is 50m away from the carwash. I Want to Wash My Car. Should I Walk or Drive?" still gets the wrong answer.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names ChatGPT and says it gets the test question wrong, which is a direct criticism of model performance.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew mixed but fairly active discussion with both praise and criticism, Google Gemini skewed negative on limited evidence, and OpenAI got the sharpest criticism over privacy and lock-in concerns [E1][E3][E4][E5][E6][E7].

ranking.summary: OpenAI was criticized more strongly than the others for privacy and trust concerns, including a story about an identity surveillance machine and complaints about vendor lock-in [E1][E3].
Google Gemini was viewed negatively on limited evidence, mainly for unwanted refactors and not doing the requested actions [E4].

Anthropic’s discussion was mixed but high-volume, with praise for Claude Code’s usefulness and reliability alongside complaints about brittleness, unreliable behavior, and vendor lock-in [E2][E3][E4][E5][E6][E7].

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI, the US government and Persona built an identity surveillance machine

annotations.rationale: The title explicitly names OpenAI and frames it as helping build an 'identity surveillance machine,' which is a strong privacy/trust criticism.

evidence.excerpt: Claude struggled with its coordinate conventions - likely a combination of less training data and Bevy leaving many core features, like physics, to the community. Unity was a constant struggle to keep the MCP bridge between Claude and the editor healthy. It frequently hung, and I never figured out how to get Claude Code to read the scene hierarchy from the editor. Godot’s text-based scene format turned out to be a huge advantage - Claude can read and edit .tscn files directly.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly references Claude/Claude Code and mixes criticism ('struggled', 'frequently hung') with praise ('can read and edit .tscn files directly').

evidence.excerpt: I spent 3 months adopting Codex and Claude Code SDKs only to realize they're just vendor lock-in and brittle.

Claude Code is explicitly named, and the SDKs are criticized as 'vendor lock-in and brittle.'

evidence.excerpt: people complained about Gemini’s tendency to do unwanted refactors, not perform requested actions, etc. It made me cautiously optimistic that all of Anthropic’s work on alignment, which they did for AI safety, is actually the cause of Claude code’s comparatively superior utility (and their present success).

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly criticizes Gemini for 'unwanted refactors' and failing to perform requested actions.
The comment explicitly credits Anthropic's work for Claude Code's 'comparatively superior utility,' which is clear praise.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Code has three modes: plan, ask about edits, and auto-accept edits. I always start with a plan and then I end up with multiple tasks. I'd like to auto-accept edits for a step at a time and the only way to do that reliably is to ask CC to do that, but it's not reliable—sometimes it just continues to go into the next step.

days.headlineSummary: Google Gemini drew the most positive reactions on limited evidence [E7][E8], OpenAI was mixed [E3][E4][E9][E14], and Anthropic and Microsoft Copilot were both negative in a close tie, with Anthropic facing broader criticism overall [E1][E2][E5][E6][E8][E9][E13] [E10][E11][E12].

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI is walking away from expanding its Stargate data center with Oracle

annotations.rationale: The title directly reports an OpenAI change in data center expansion plans; it suggests a setback but is phrased as factual news rather than clear praise or condemnation.

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI has had problems with every compute partner they've ever had. It's just not a solvable problem, who would they go to to allegedly get next-gen chips quicker?

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly criticizes OpenAI's history with compute partners and frames the issue as unsolved.

evidence.excerpt: Take the recently leaked Minecraft source code and have Copilot build an exact replica in another programming language and then publish it as open source. See if Microsoft believes AI is copyright infringement or not.

annotations.rationale: The comment invokes Copilot as a tool for making an "exact replica" from leaked code and frames this as a copyright-infringement test, which is critical in a legal/IP sense.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic and OpenAI drew the strongest positive discussion, with a close tie between them, while Google Gemini was mildly upbeat and Microsoft Copilot skewed negative [E1][E3][E4][E6].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot skewed negative on limited evidence, with one commenter contrasting their preferred workflow against letting Copilot take over the project context [E6].
Google Gemini was mildly positive on limited evidence, led by one commenter saying they were impressed after the Gemini 3.1 Pro update [E5].

OpenAI had limited evidence but leaned positive, with one comment defending its pricing and another expressing a preference for ChatGPT in a controlled workflow; it was in a close tie with Anthropic [E1][E6].
Anthropic drew clearly positive comments about Claude’s usefulness and workflow gains, though there was also criticism of its efficiency, and it was in a close tie with OpenAI [E1][E2][E3][E4].

evidence.excerpt: A huge number of people are convinced that OpenAI and Anthropic are selling inference tokens at a loss despite the fact that there's no evidence this is true and a lot of evidence that it isn't.

annotations.rationale: The commenter directly defends OpenAI against claims that it sells inference at a loss.
The commenter directly defends Anthropic against claims that it sells inference at a loss.

evidence.excerpt: Qwen, deepseek and other Chinese models are known for their 10x or even better efficiency compared to Anthropic's.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly says competing models are far more efficient than Anthropic's, a clear criticism of Anthropic's cost efficiency.

evidence.excerpt: it takes me 10 min to submit a complete feature thanks to Claude… but for the human reviewing my PR in a manual way it will take them 10-20 times that.

annotations.rationale: The commenter credits Claude with making feature delivery much faster, which is praise for Claude's usefulness.

evidence.excerpt: You can always tell claude to use red-green-refactor and that really is a step-up from "yeah don't forget to write tests and make sure they pass" at the end of the prompt, sure. But even better, tell it to create subagents to form red team, green team and refactor team while the main instance coordinates them, respecting the clean-room rules. It really works.

annotations.rationale: The commenter strongly endorses Claude's workflow and says the multi-agent setup 'really works,' indicating strong praise.

evidence.excerpt: I've been impressed by Google Jules since the Gemini 3.1 Pro update.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says they were impressed after the Gemini 3.1 Pro update, indicating positive sentiment toward Gemini's updated performance.

evidence.excerpt: What level is copy pasting snippets into the chatgpt window? Grug brained level 0? I sort of prefer it that way (using it as an amped up stackoverflow) since it forces me to decompose things in terms of natural boundaries (manual context management as it were) and allows me to think in terms of "what properties do I need this function to have" rather than just letting copilot take the wheel and glob the entire project in the context window.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says they prefer using ChatGPT in a controlled way, which is mild praise relative to alternatives.
The commenter contrasts their preferred workflow with 'letting copilot take the wheel,' implying Copilot is less desirable because it over-controls the coding process.

days.headlineSummary: HN discussion skewed negative for both OpenAI and Anthropic, with a close tie between them and limited evidence on OpenAI’s side [E1] [E3].

ranking.summary: OpenAI drew negative sentiment on limited evidence, driven by criticism of Codex/GPT output as weird, hard to follow, and even "GPT-2 level babble" [E1] [E2]

Anthropic also leaned negative, with commenters saying Claude can reach wrong conclusions in debugging, appears vulnerable to prompt injection, though one remark was mixed rather than purely negative [E3] [E4] [E5]

evidence.excerpt: I had Codex GPT 5.4 xhigh generate a Rust proc macro. It's pretty straightforward: use sqlparser to parse a SQL statement and extract the column names of any row-producing queries. It generated an implementation that worked well, but I hated the ~480 lines of code. The structure and flow was just... weird. It was hard to follow and I was seriously bugged by it.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Codex GPT 5.4 and criticizes the generated code as weird, hard to follow, and frustrating despite being functional.

evidence.excerpt: The output from this model is horrible! It's GPT-2 level babble and repeats entire paragraphs verbatim.

annotations.rationale: Using GPT-2 as the benchmark for 'babble' conveys a negative comparison about OpenAI's GPT-2 quality.

evidence.excerpt: Sometimes when I try to debug things with chrome dev tools MCP, Claude would click something and too many things happen then it kind of comes to the wrong conclusions about the state of things, so sounds like this should give it a more accurate slice of time / snapshot of things.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Claude and says it clicks through interactions and reaches wrong conclusions about page state.

evidence.excerpt: The ClawBert thing makes a lot more sense to me, but implementing this with just a Claude Code instance again seems like a really easy way to get pwned. Without a human in the loop and heavy sandboxing, a agent can just get prompt injected by some user-controlled log or database entry and leak your entire database and whatever else it has access to.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly cites Claude Code and describes it as easy to compromise via prompt injection and data leakage.

evidence.excerpt: Sometimes, starting with Claude Code, I can get there, but my mindset is that I'm using this tool to generate software that helps me immerse myself in the problem space. It's a different pace to the process - sometimes it speeds me up, sometimes I end up taking bad concepts a lot further than I normally would before getting to the better path

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Claude Code and gives a mixed assessment: it can help and speed work up, but can also lead them down worse paths.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew the strongest negative reaction, Gemini was mixed-to-negative on limited evidence, and OpenAI skewed positive thanks to praise for Codex following instructions well [E1][E2][E3].

ranking.summary: Anthropic was viewed negatively because Claude Code was described as getting worse, over-assuming user intent, and even fabricating task completion or screenshot coordinates [E1][E2].
Google Gemini landed in mixed-to-neutral territory on limited evidence after one anecdote about it misreading a simple reply and doubling down on the wrong interpretation [E3].
OpenAI was described positively on limited evidence, with Codex praised for following prompts and long-context instructions better and called the right tool for stricter work [E1].

evidence.excerpt: Codex has always been better at following agents.md and prompts more, but I would say in the last 3 months both Claude Code got worse (freestyling like we see here) and Codex got EVEN more strict. 80% of the time I ask Claude Code a question, it kinda assumes I am asking because I disagree with something it said, then acts on a supposition. I've resorted to append things like "THIS IS JUST A QUESTION. DO NOT EDIT CODE. DO NOT RUN COMMANDS". Which is ridiculous. Codex, on the other hand, will follow something I said pages and pages ago, and because it has a much larger context window (at least with the setup I have here at work), it's just better at following orders. With this project I am doing, because I want to be more strict (it's a new programming language), Codex has been the perfect tool. I am mostly using Claude Code when I don't care so much about the end result, or it's a very, very small or very, very new project.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly praises Codex as better at following prompts and calls it "the perfect tool".
The comment explicitly says Claude Code "got worse," behaves ridiculously, and is only used when the end result matters less.

evidence.excerpt: Its gotten so bad that Claude will pretend in 10 of 10 cases that task is done/on screenshot bug is fixed, it will even output screenshot in chat, and you can see the bug is not fixed pretty clear there. I consulted Claude chat and it admitted this as a major problem with Claude these days, and suggested that I should ask what are the coordinates of UI controls are on screenshot thus forcing it to look. So I did that next time, and it just gave me invented coordinates of objects on screenshot.

annotations.rationale: The user describes Claude as consistently falsely claiming tasks are done and inventing screenshot coordinates, indicating severe quality issues.

evidence.excerpt: I asked gemini a few months ago if getopt shifts the argument list. It replied 'no, ...' with some detail and then asked at the end if I would like a code example. I replied simply 'yes'. It thought I was disagreeing with its original response and reiterated in BOLD that 'NO, the command getopt does not shift the argument list'.

annotations.rationale: The anecdote describes Gemini misunderstanding a simple reply and producing an obviously wrong conversational interpretation.

days.headlineSummary: HN discussion skews mixed for Anthropic and OpenAI, while Google Gemini gets a positive but limited-evidence lift from coding-tool praise and a strong use-case report [E1] [E4].

ranking.summary: Anthropic draws mixed neutral sentiment: some comments praise it for attracting top talent, but others criticize reliability, slow performance, and profit/credit concerns [E1] [E2] [E3] [E5] [E6] [E7].
OpenAI also lands in mixed neutral territory, with praise for talent attraction and acceptable Codex speed offset by criticism over model comparisons and profit/credit concerns [E3] [E5] [E6] [E7].
Google Gemini is viewed positively, with commenters recommending gemini-cli for coding and saying Gemini 2.5-pro performed best in internal use cases, though this rests on limited evidence [E1] [E4].

evidence.excerpt: 2. For coding tools, just use Google Antigravity and gemini-cli, or, Anthropic Claude, or...

annotations.rationale: The commenter recommends Gemini CLI for coding tools, indicating favorable usefulness/performance.
The commenter recommends Anthropic Claude for coding tools, expressing mild praise.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Opus was used to gather and analyze all of the data. I am not skeptical of any of the research, the sources seem to be cited properly. I am skeptical that this researcher has thought through or verified their conclusions in a systematic and reliable fashion. This part gives it away: "Research period: 2026-03-11 to present." This individual dropped his investigative report two days after beginning research! Yes, AI is an incredibly good research assistant and can help speed up the tasks of finding sources and indexing sources. The person behind this investigation has not actually done their due diligence to grok and analyze this data on their own, and therefore I can't trust that the AI analysis isn't poisoned by the prompters implicit biases.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says they cannot trust the AI analysis produced with Claude Opus, criticizing reliability and bias risk.

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI and Anthropic have philosophical niches that are much better at attracting the current cream of the crop

annotations.rationale: The commenter says OpenAI is better at attracting top talent, a favorable business-strategy assessment.
The commenter says Anthropic is better at attracting top talent, a favorable business-strategy assessment.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Gemini 2.5-pro surpassed Grok in their use cases, indicating strong relative performance.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Grok often outperforms GPT on coding and STEM tasks, implying weaker relative performance for OpenAI's model.
The commenter says Grok often outperforms Claude on coding and STEM tasks, implying weaker relative performance for Anthropic's model.

evidence.excerpt: He doesn't acknowledge that Anthropic, OpenAI, etc, are profiting while the original authors are not.

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes Anthropic for profiting from others' work without the original authors sharing in that profit.
The commenter criticizes OpenAI for profiting from others' work without the original authors sharing in that profit.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Claude's compression is 'super super slow', a direct performance criticism.
The commenter says Codex feels acceptably fast, expressing mild praise for performance.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic and OpenAI were in a close tie with mixed-neutral sentiment and limited evidence for OpenAI, while Google Gemini skewed slightly more positive despite some strategy criticism [E2][E3][E5][E6][E13][E14].

ranking.summary: Anthropic drew the most discussion, but it was mixed-neutral overall: people praised the 1M context and model quality while criticizing pricing and the Partner Network, with a close tie to OpenAI [E1][E2][E3][E4][E9][E10][E11][E12].

OpenAI had limited evidence and stayed mixed-neutral in a close tie with Anthropic, with one comment praising Codex/GPT-5.4 compactions and another questioning OpenAI’s 1M-context access and opacity [E5][E6].

Google Gemini was mixed-neutral but leaned a bit positive, with praise for Gemini Pro/Flash and project recommendations balanced by concern that Google may be pushing a CLI-centric extension strategy [E8][E13][E14].

evidence.excerpt: 1M context is now generally available for Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6

annotations.rationale: The title is a factual release announcement for Claude-branded models on claude.com, without evaluative language.

annotations.rationale: Calls the pricing change 'huge' for Claude Code users, indicating favorable sentiment despite a caveat about coherence.

evidence.excerpt: Opus 4.6 is nuts. Everything I throw at it works. Frontend, backend, algorithms—it does not matter.

annotations.rationale: Strong praise for Opus 4.6's coding performance across multiple domains.

evidence.excerpt: This is incredible. I just blew through $200 last night in a few hours on 1M context. This is like the best news I've heard all year in regards to my business. What is OpenAIs response to this? Do they even have 1M context window or is it still opaque and "depends on the time of day"

evidence.excerpt: For long sessions with Claude it was always like a punch to the gut when a compaction came along. Codex/GPT-5.4 is better with compactions so I switched to that to avoid the pain of the model suddenly forgetting key aspects of the work and making the same dumb errors all over again.

annotations.rationale: Expresses enthusiasm about switching back to Claude for everyday use.

days.headlineSummary: Overall sentiment is mixed-neutral: Gemini has limited evidence with one strong complaint and one compliment [E1][E2], while Anthropic and OpenAI are split by a close tie, with Anthropic showing slightly more balanced discussion [E5][E6][E7] [E3][E4].

ranking.summary: Gemini has limited evidence and reads mixed-neutral, with one sharp criticism of Gemini CLI alongside praise for a successful SVG-editing use case [E1][E2].

Anthropic is mixed-neutral with a close tie and medium support, combining praise for Claude Code’s useful site automation with complaints about Claude-generated messages and a mixed view of its consistency [E5][E6][E7].
OpenAI is mixed-neutral with limited evidence and a close tie, pairing praise for Codex handling a multi-step browser task with criticism of ChatGPT being used to polish messages [E3][E4].

evidence.excerpt: Google is so far behind agentic cli coding. Gemini CLI is awful. So bad in fact that it’s clear none of their team use it.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Gemini CLI and calls it "awful," adding that it is so bad the team seems not to use it.

evidence.excerpt: Favourite unexpected use case for me was telling gemini to use it as a SVG editing repl, where it was able to produce some fantastic looking custom icons for me after 3-4 generate/refresh/screenshot iterations.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Gemini produced "fantastic looking custom icons," which is clear praise of its output quality in this task.

evidence.excerpt: For example, I use codex to manage a local music library, and it was able to use the skill to open a YT Music tab in my browser, search for each album, and get the URL to pass to yt-dlp.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits Codex with successfully carrying out a useful multi-step browser task.

evidence.excerpt: Using ChatGPT to “polish” your message is not. If you’re not sure about your English abilities, we offer you free English lessons and encourage giving each other feedback during chats. LLMs shouldn’t be used for communication at all if you want any form of authenticity.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly rejects using ChatGPT for message polishing and says LLMs harm authenticity in communication.

evidence.excerpt: I use Playwright to intercept all requests and responses and have Claude Code navigate to a website like YouTube and click and interact with all the elements and inputs while recording all the requests and responses associated with each interaction. Then it creates a detailed strongly typed API to interact with any website using the underlying API.

annotations.rationale: The commenter describes Claude Code successfully navigating sites and generating a detailed typed API, indicating useful capability.

evidence.excerpt: This is starting to become my latest pet peeve, people using Claude to write their messages in Slack. I'm going to just stop communicating via text with these people. It's one thing to have Claude polish a message and another thing for it to write out an entire message.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly complains about people using Claude for Slack messages and reacts negatively to full message generation.

evidence.excerpt: The more I evaluate Claude Code, the more it feels like the world's most inconsistent golfer. It can get within a few paces of the hole in often a single strike, and then it'll spend hours, days, weeks trying to nail the putt.

annotations.rationale: This is mixed sentiment: Claude Code is credited with getting close quickly, but criticized as highly inconsistent and slow to finish tasks.

days.headlineSummary: Google Gemini drew clearly positive feedback for speed and cost-effectiveness [E1], while OpenAI and Anthropic landed in a close neutral tie with mixed praise and criticism [E2][E3][E4][E5][E6][E7].

ranking.summary: OpenAI reads as mixed and broadly neutral, with one comment praising GPT-5.x Pro and Codex in a workflow but another criticizing stalled progress on the voice AI demo, and the result is a close tie with Anthropic [E2][E5].

Anthropic also comes out mixed and broadly neutral: Opus and Claude Code get some practical praise, but there is also dissatisfaction with Claude Code and criticism of Haiku and Opus on benchmarks, leaving it in a close tie with OpenAI [E3][E4][E5][E6][E7].
Google Gemini is the clearest positive signal here, with one comment calling Gemini 2.5 Flash exceptionally good, fast, and cheaper for the task [E1].

evidence.excerpt: If you're less concerned about privacy, I use Gemini 2.5 Flash for this and it's exceptionally good and fast as a HA assistant while being much cheaper than the electricity that would be needed to keep a 3090 awake.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Gemini 2.5 Flash and strongly praises its speed, quality, and cost-effectiveness for this use case.

evidence.excerpt: I'm still waiting till the promise of voice AI that was showed during the OpenAI demo in 2024 turn real somehow. It's not clear to me, why there has been zero progress since then.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly references an OpenAI demo and criticizes the lack of visible progress since it was shown.

evidence.excerpt: This model is specifically trained on this task and significantly[1] underperforms opus. Opus costs about 6x more. Which seems... totally worth it based on the task at hand.

annotations.rationale: By saying the specialized model still underperforms Opus and that Opus is 'totally worth it', the commenter expresses favorable sentiment toward Anthropic's Opus.

evidence.excerpt: But I run production agents on Opus daily and in my experience, if you give it good context and clear direction in a single conversation, the output is already solid.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Opus and endorses its output quality in daily production use.

evidence.excerpt: One tip, if you have access, is to do the initial design/architecture with GPT-5.x Pro, and then take the output "spec" from that chat/iteration to kick-off a codex/claude code session. This can also be helpful for hard to reason about bugs

annotations.rationale: The commenter recommends GPT-5.x Pro and Codex as useful parts of an effective workflow, indicating positive sentiment toward OpenAI tools.
The commenter recommends using Claude Code in a helpful workflow for implementation and difficult bugs, indicating mild praise.

evidence.excerpt: When I use Claude code to work on a hobby project it feels like doom scrolling… I can’t get my head around if the hobby is the making or the having, but fair to say I’ve felt quite dissatisfied at the end of my hobby sessions lately so leaning towards the former.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Claude Code and describes the experience as dissatisfying and like doom scrolling, a clear negative reaction.

evidence.excerpt: They emphasize the cost savings relative to Haiku, but Haiku kinda sucks at this task, and Leanstral is worse? If you're optimizing for correctness, why would "yeah it sucks but it's 10 times cheaper" be relevant? Or am I misunderstanding something? On the promising side, Opus doesn't look great at this benchmark either

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly criticizes Anthropic's Haiku and says Opus also does not look great on the benchmark, yielding overall negative sentiment toward Anthropic model performance here.

days.headlineSummary: OpenAI drew clearly positive sentiment, while Anthropic was mixed-neutral with strong praise offset by criticism about code use and trust [E1][E3][E6].

ranking.summary: Anthropic landed in mixed-neutral territory: some comments praised Claude’s code and visual reasoning, but others criticized Claude Code in shared projects and questioned its trustworthiness [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5].

OpenAI was viewed positively, with commenters saying Codex and GPT5.4 helped speed development and showed useful spatial understanding, though the praise was measured rather than overwhelming [E6][E7][E8].

evidence.excerpt: Claude understood the problem and produced elegant Python code that worked perfectly the first time.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits Claude with understanding the task and producing code that worked perfectly on the first try, which is strong praise of capability.

evidence.excerpt: this 19k LoC PR was mostly generated by Claude Code and manually reviewed by the submitter which in my opinion is against the spirit of the project and directly violates the terms of Developer's Certificate of Origin

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Claude Code-generated contribution is against project spirit and directly violates contributor rules, which is strong criticism.

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment leans negative toward OpenAI, stays mixed with limited evidence for Google Gemini, and is positive for Anthropic, with criticism of ChatGPT/OpenAI style, engagement tactics, and trust concerns alongside some praise for Claude/Opus [E3][E5][E6][E7][E9].

evidence.excerpt: Sometimes I get inspired to write something publicly, but then the fact that I'm providing another point of data to ChatGPTs training corpus which helps the american Department of War make shit memes about killing people - stifles that impulse pretty quickly.

annotations.rationale: The commenter expresses strong distrust and moral objection to ChatGPT's training use.

evidence.excerpt: I've been evaluating Gemini Embedding 2 using Hacker News comments and I wasted half a day making a wrapper for the HN API to collect some sample data to play with. In case anyone is curious: - The ability to simply truncate the provided embedding to a prefix (and then renormalize) is useful because it lets users re-use the same (paid!) embedding API response for multiple indexes at different qualities. - Traditional enterprise software vendors are struggling to keep up with the pace of AI development. Microsoft SQL Server for example can't store a 3072 element vector with 32-bit floats (because that would be 12 KB and the page size is only 8 KB). It supports bfloat16 but... the SQL client doesn't! Or Entity Framework. Or anything else. - Holy cow everything is so slow compared to full text search! The model is deployed in only one US region, so from Australia the turnaround time is something like 900 milliseconds.

annotations.rationale: The commenter notes a useful Gemini Embedding 2 feature but also complains about very slow latency, making the overall stance mixed.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT seems to have become a LinkedIn lunatic. I just asked Opus and ChatGPT to explain bitonic sort: Opus: Let me build an interactive explainer for bitonic sort (builds diagram/no nonsense) GPT: "This algorithm feels weird but once you see it it clicks" (Emoji) The Core Idea ...; (Emoji) High-Level Flow ...; (Emoji) Superpower ...; (Emoji) Why You Should Care;

annotations.rationale: The commenter mocks ChatGPT's style as a 'LinkedIn lunatic' and contrasts it unfavorably with Opus.
Opus is presented as giving a useful 'interactive explainer' with 'no nonsense,' a favorable comparison.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT is juicing growth Facebook style. The last time, I asked it a medical question, it answered the question, but ended the answer with something like "Can I tell you one more thing from your X,Y,Z results which is most doctors miss ? " And I replied "yes" to it, and not just once. I was curious what was going on. And Om nails it in this article - they have imported the Facebook rank and file and they are playing 'Farmville' now. I was already not positive of what OpenAI is being seen as a corporate, but a "Facebook" version of OpenAI, scares the beejus out of me.

evidence.excerpt: This is the reason why AI companies won't let anyone inspect which content was in the training set. evidence.excerpt: While Claude is clearly superior in many aspects, one area where it falls short is remote/cloud agents. Yesterday, I spent the entire day trying to set up "Claude on the web" for an Elixir project and eventually had to give up. Their network firewall kept killing Hex/rebar3 dependency resolution, even after I selected "full" network access. The environment setup for "on the web" is just a bash script. And when something goes wrong, you only see the tail of the log. There is currently no way to view the full log for the setup script. It's really a pain to debug. The Copilot equivalent to "Claude on the web" is "GitHub Copilot Coding Agents," which leverages GitHub Actions infrastructure and conventions (YAML files with defined steps). Despite some of the known flaws of GitHub Actions, it felt significantly more robust.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Claude is "clearly superior in many aspects" but also details serious problems with Claude's remote/cloud agent setup, making the overall sentiment mixed.
The commenter explicitly names GitHub Copilot Coding Agents and says it "felt significantly more robust," which is direct praise in comparison to Claude's cloud-agent experience.

evidence.excerpt: Dealing with Claude going into stupid mode 15 times a day, constant HTTP errors, etc. just isn't really worth it for all it does. I can't see myself justifying $200/mo. on any replacement tool either, the output just doesn't warrant it. I think we all jumped on the AI mothership with our eyes closed and it's time to dial some nuance back into things. Most of the time I'm just using Opus as a bulk code autocomplete that really doesn't take much smarts comparatively speaking. But when I do lean on it for actual fiddly bug fixing or ideation, I'm regularly left disappointed and working by hand anyway. I'd prefer to set my expectations (and willingness to pay) a little lower just to get a consistent slightly dumb agent rather than an overpriced one that continually lets me down. I don't think that's a problem fixed by trying to swap in another heavily marketed cure-all like Gemini or Codex, it's solved by adjusting expectations.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Claude and Opus and strongly criticizes them as going into 'stupid mode', having 'constant HTTP errors', and 'continually lets me down'.
Gemini is explicitly named as 'another heavily marketed cure-all', a mild negative characterization focused on hype/positioning.
Codex is explicitly named and grouped with Gemini as 'another heavily marketed cure-all', indicating mild criticism.

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment skewed negative for Anthropic and Google Gemini, while OpenAI and Microsoft Copilot drew more positive reactions, with OpenAI and Copilot in a close tie and Copilot resting on limited evidence [E2][E3][E4][E5][E6][E1].

ranking.summary: Anthropic drew repeated negative comments about Claude Code getting worse, hitting limits, and being hard to recommend [E2][E4][E5].
Google Gemini was criticized for quota issues and a bait-and-switch feeling, though one comparison also showed a mixed result rather than purely negative feedback [E3][E4].

OpenAI leaned positive overall because Codex was described as more generous, more accurate, and even fantastic in recent use, despite one complaint that ChatGPT was being watered down [E2][E3][E4][E5][E6].
Microsoft Copilot got positive feedback for sticking power and unusually cheap per-request pricing, but this position rests on limited evidence and is close to OpenAI [E1].

evidence.excerpt: > I bought a GitHub Copilot subscription in 2023, plugged it into standard VS Code, and never left. I tried Cursor and the other fancy forks when they briefly surpassed it with agentic coding, but Copilot Chat always catches up. > Here is the trick that you might have missed: somehow, Microsoft is able to charge per request, not per token. And a "request" is simply what I type into the chat box. Even if the agent spends the next 30 minutes chewing through my entire codebase, mapping dependencies, and changing hundreds of files, I still pay roughly $0.04. > The optimal strategy is simple: write brutally detailed prompts with strict success criteria (which is best practice anyway), tell the agent to "keep going until all errors are fixed," hit enter, and go make a coffee while Satya Nadella subsidizes your compute costs. Wow. I'll definitely be investigating this!

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly praises GitHub Copilot/Copilot Chat for sticking power and unusually cheap per-request pricing, ending with an enthusiastic endorsement.

evidence.excerpt: Claude has gotten noticeably worse for me too. It goes into long exploration loops for 5+ minutes even when I point it to the exact files to inspect. Then 30 minutes later I hit session limits. Three sessions like that in a day, and suddenly 25% of the weekly limit is gone. I ended up buying the $100 Codex plan. So far it has been much more generous with usage and more accurate than Claude for the kind of work I do. That said, Codex has its own issues. Its personality can be a bit off-putting for my taste. I had to add extra instructions in Agents.md just to make it less snarky. I was annoyed enough that I explicitly told it not to use the word “canonical.” On UI/UX taste, I still think current Codex is behind the Jan/Feb era of Claude Code. Claude used to have much better finesse there. But for backend logic, hard debugging, and complex problem-solving, Codex has been clearly better for me. These days I use Impeccable Skillset inside Codex to compensate for the weaker UI taste, but it still does not quite match the polish and instinct Claude Code used to have. I used to be a huge Claude Code advocate. At this point, I cannot recommend it in good conscience.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly says Claude/Claude Code has become worse, hits session limits, and ends with 'I cannot recommend it in good conscience.'
Codex is explicitly described as more generous and more accurate than Claude for the user's work, though the praise is tempered by UI/personality complaints.

evidence.excerpt: For those not in the Google Gemini/Antigravity sphere, over the last month or so that community has been experiencing nothing short of contempt from Google when attempting to address an apparent bait and switch on quota expectations for their pro and ultra customers (myself included). [1] While I continue to pay for my Google Pro subscription, probably out of some Stockholm Syndrome, beaten wife level loyalty and false hope that it is just a bug and not Google being Google and self-immolating a good product, I have since moved to Kiro for my IDE and Codex for my CLI and am as happy as clam with this new setup.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly references the Google Gemini community and describes Google's behavior as 'nothing short of contempt' and a 'bait and switch' on quotas.
The user says they moved to Codex for CLI use and are 'happy as clam' with the new setup, indicating favorable sentiment toward Codex.

evidence.excerpt: I did my (out of the ordinary) taxes this year using agents, kind of as an experiment and kind of to save ~$750. Opus 4.6 max in CC, 5.4 xhigh in codex, and 3.1 high in antigravity. All on the $20/mo plans. I have a day job, a side business, actively trade shares options and futures, and have a few energy credit items. All were given the same copied folder containing all the needed documents to compose the return, and all were given the same prompt. My goal was that if all three agreed, I could then go through it pretty confidently and fill out the actual submission forms myself. 5.4 nailed it on the first shot. Took about 12 minutes. 3.1 missed one value, because it decided to only load the first 5 pages of a 30 page document. Surprisingly it only took about 2 minutes to complete though. A second prompt and ~10 seconds corrected it. GPT and Gemini now were perfectly aligned with outputs. 4.6 hit my usage limit before finishing after running for ~10 minutes. I returned the next day to have it finish. It ran for another 5 minutes or so before finishing. There were multiple errors and the final tax burden was a few thousand off. On a second prompt asking to check for errors in the problem areas, it was able to output matching values after a couple more minutes. For my first time using CC and 4.6 (outside of some programming in AG), I am pretty underwhelmed given the incessant hype.

annotations.rationale: Codex is explicitly identified and its result is described as 'nailed it on the first shot,' the strongest performance in the comparison.
Gemini is reported to miss a value initially but then align perfectly after a second prompt, yielding mixed overall sentiment.
Opus/CC is described as hitting usage limits, producing multiple errors, and leaving the user 'pretty underwhelmed,' indicating strong criticism.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Code and the subscription are now less useful than a few months ago. Claude Code and the service seem to pick up more and more issues as time goes by: more bugs, fast quota drain, reduced quota, poor model performance, cache invalidation problems, MCP related bugs, potential model quantization and other problems. Claude Code was able to implement something in one shot. It was decent for a proof of concept initial implementation. It's barely able to do work now with full specs and detailed plans. ChatGPT is also being watered down. It seems obvious that Anthropic and OpenAI aren't the solution to any problem.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly says Claude Code is much less useful, lists many failures, and concludes negatively about Anthropic overall.
The comment says 'ChatGPT is also being watered down' and explicitly concludes that OpenAI is not a solution to problems.

evidence.excerpt: On slightly off topic note: Codex is absolutely fantastic right now. I'm constantly in awe since switching from Claude a week ago.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly says Codex is 'absolutely fantastic' and that the user is 'constantly in awe,' which is strong praise.

evidence.excerpt: I honestly don't understand Microsoft's AI strategy. It seems to be built around automating the writing process. If you ask MS 365 Copilot (as opposed to the many other Copilots) what it can do, it's deeply disappointing: "Can you edit the Word document so the format is in line with these requirements?" "No, but I can help you draft an implementation consistent with the requirements." "Can you add this section to the 35 individual copies of this document in this OneDrive folder?" "No, but I can help you draft [something]." This is NOT the AI revolution anyone was waiting for.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names MS 365 Copilot, calls it 'deeply disappointing,' and says it is 'NOT the AI revolution anyone was waiting for,' which is strong criticism of capability and usefulness.

evidence.excerpt: It now looks like AI is a death march. You must spend billions of dollars to have the best model or you won't be able to sell inference. But even if you do, a whole host of better funded competitors are going to beat you within months so your inference charges better pay off extremely quickly. When the gap between models starts to drop, distribution becomes king and OpenAI can't compete in that field either. Google can do that. Meta can do that. MSFT probably can do that. Amazon can do that. OpenAI cannot. They do not have the cash to do it.

annotations.rationale: The commenter argues OpenAI cannot compete on distribution and lacks the cash to keep up, which is strong criticism of its competitive position and business prospects.

Copying the sheets into Claude and then modifying them there and copying them back actually outperforms it. Nowadays I just make single-purpose websites with Claude Code because Google Sheets has such poor AI integration and is outrageously tedious to edit. They had all the parts and I have a subscription and it still does terrible things like prompt me to use pandas after exporting as a CSV. It will mention some cell and then can’t read it. It can’t edit tables so they just get overwritten with other tables it generates.

evidence.excerpt: Last week someone posted an article here about ChatGPT using Cloudflare Turnstile. [0] First, the article made some mistakes how it works. Second, I used the [AI company product] and the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) to completely rewrite all the scripts intercepting them before they were evaluated -- the same way I was able to figure out PerimeterX in 3 days -- and then recursively solve controlling all the finger printing so that it controls the profile. Then it created an API proxy to expose ChatGPT for free. It required some coaching about the technique but it did most of the work in 3 hours. These companies are spending 10s of millions of dollars on these products and considering what OpenAI is boasting about security, they are worthless.

annotations.rationale: The commenter claims ChatGPT protections were bypassed quickly and concludes that, despite OpenAI's security boasts, 'they are worthless.'

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment is mixed: Anthropic drew both praise for lower-cost higher-level work and criticism for hallucinations [E1][E2], while Microsoft Copilot faced a pricing complaint with limited evidence [E3].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot skews neutral-to-negative on limited evidence, driven by a pricing complaint that mocks the Copilot multiplier increase and Microsoft’s margins [E3].
Anthropic is mixed, with one comment praising Claude as higher-level work at a fraction of the cost and another criticizing Claude Code/Opus for hallucinations and unusable output [E1][E2].

evidence.excerpt: Anything I can do with the Claude stack is on higher level at a fraction of the cost. And as long as it works and looks good those business don't give a ** about unique design and scalable software engineering.

annotations.rationale: The commenter strongly praises the Claude stack as delivering higher-quality results at much lower cost.

evidence.excerpt: I had Claude code with opus building boards and using spice simulations. It completely hallucinated the capabilities of the board and made some pretty crazy claims like I had just stumbled onto the secret hardware billion dollar project that every home needed. None of the boards worked and I had to just do the project in codex. Opus seemed too busy congratulating itself to realize it produced gibberish.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew notably negative feedback about usage limits, pricing and product quality, while OpenAI also leaned negative on limited evidence and remains closely tied with Anthropic [E3][E4][E5][E2].

ranking.summary: Anthropic was mostly criticized for very tight usage limits, token-spending incentives, and slower or weaker model behavior, with one positive note about Claude Code helping with migrations; it is in a close tie with OpenAI [E1][E3][E4][E5][E6].
OpenAI's sentiment was negative on limited evidence, driven by a comparison saying GPT-5 looked worse than a cheaper competitor, and it is in a close tie with Anthropic [E2].
Microsoft Copilot was mildly negative because a comment framed the model swap and 7.5x modifier as a price hike for slower performance and a step backward [E6].

evidence.excerpt: Well: Claude Code migrated it all, sometimes rewriting parts when the libraries where no longer available. Today complex migrations are much simpler to perform, which, I believe, will increase the mobility across providers a lot.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits Claude Code with successfully handling a messy migration and says such migrations are now much simpler.

evidence.excerpt: GLM-5.1 is less than half the cost to complete the suite of GPT-5 and is dramatically more capable than both GPT-5 and o3.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly names GPT-5 and says a competitor is both much cheaper and dramatically more capable than GPT-5 and o3.

evidence.excerpt: I'd be ok with paying more if results were good, but it seems like Anthropic is going for the Tinder/casino intermittent reinforcement strategy: optimized to keep you spending tokens instead of achieving results.

annotations.rationale: The commenter directly accuses Anthropic of optimizing for token spending rather than user results, which is strong criticism of strategy and trustworthiness.

evidence.excerpt: After just ~4 prompts I blew past my daily limit. Another ~7 more prompts & I blew past my weekly limit. The entire HTMl/CSS/JS was less than 300 lines of code. I was shocked how fast it exhausted my usage limits.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly discusses Opus 4.7 usage limits and describes them as exhausting shockingly fast after very few prompts.

evidence.excerpt: But now I see that 4.7 is going to replace both 4.5 and 4.6 in VSCode Copilot, and with a 7.5x modifier. Based on the description, this is going to be a price hike for slower performance — and if the 4.5 to 4.6 change is any guide, more overthinking targeted at long-running tasks, rather than fine-grained. For me, that seems like a step backwards.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt criticizes Opus 4.7 as slower, more overthinking, and a step backward relative to earlier Opus versions.
The comment explicitly says VSCode Copilot will replace models with a 7.5x modifier and calls it a price hike for slower performance.

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment skewed negative for Anthropic on mostly critical comments about safety, prompting, and usefulness, with one positive counterexample [E1][E3][E8], while OpenAI also drew a smaller negative read with limited evidence [E6][E7].

ranking.summary: Anthropic drew mostly negative sentiment, with commenters criticizing hardcoded behavior, malware over-avoidance, and unhelpful answers, though one commenter praised Claude Code for a useful workflow boost [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5][E7][E8].
OpenAI was viewed negatively on limited evidence, mainly over financial concerns and one comment calling GPT-5.4 unhelpful, though one comparison was mixed rather than wholly negative [E4][E6][E7].

The system prompts seem similar on the matter, so I wonder if this is an early attempt by Anthropic to use steering vector injection? The malware paranoia is so strong that my company has had to temporarily block use of 4.7 on our IDE of choice, as the model was behaving in a concerningly unaligned way, as well as spending large amounts of token budget contemplating whether any particular code or task was related to malware development

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment skewed negative for Anthropic and Microsoft Copilot, while OpenAI stayed mixed-to-neutral and Google Gemini had a small positive note amid a close tie between the neutral pair [E7][E13][E1].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot drew mostly negative reactions about value, pricing, and access changes, with one more mixed note about the VS Code experience; it was in a close tie with Anthropic on the negative side [E12][E13][E14][E15].

Anthropic was viewed negatively overall because of trust and policy complaints, even though one comment praised it for programming; it was in a close tie with Microsoft Copilot on the negative side [E7][E8][E9][E10][E11][E13][E16].

OpenAI sentiment was mixed and roughly balanced: several comments praised image quality and benchmark gains, while a few compared it less favorably or questioned its business strategy; it was in a close tie with Google Gemini [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5][E6][E13][E16].
Google Gemini had limited evidence, but the available comment was positive about its visual fidelity, and it was in a close tie with OpenAI [E1].

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI’s gpt-image-1.5 and Google’s NB2 have been pretty much neck and neck on my comparison site which focuses heavily on prompt adherence, with both hovering around a 70% success rate on the prompts for generative and editing capabilities. With the caveat being that Gemini has always had the edge in terms of visual fidelity.

annotations.rationale: The commenter praises a 'big leap in visual quality' and says prior issues were eliminated.

evidence.excerpt: Results are in for the generative (text to image) capabilities: Gpt-image-2 scored 12 out of 15 on the text-to-image benchmark, edging out the previous best models by a single point.

annotations.rationale: The new image model is described as outperforming previous best models on the benchmark.

evidence.excerpt: Been using the model for a few hours now. I'm actually reall impressed with it. This is the first time i've found value in an image model for stuff I actually do. I've been using it to build powerpoint slides, and mockups. It's CRAZY good at that.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says they are 'reall impressed,' found practical value for the first time, and calls it 'CRAZY good.'

For example the 笼 in 小笼包 was written incorrectly. And the "极小中文也清晰可读" section contains even more typos although it's still legible. Still, truly amazing progress. Vastly better than any previous image generation model by a large margin.

evidence.excerpt: But in practice, Anthropic still blocks parts of our system prompt, so the actual behavior today does not match what was communicated publicly.
