annotations.rationale: The commenter calls Anthropic unreliable and recommends leaving for other providers.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly says Anthropic is 'the best for programming,' which is favorable on coding/model quality.

OpenAI is named as one of the providers delivering '99% of the value,' which is favorable in the comparison.

The rhetorical question suggests OpenAI may be financially pressured and acting defensively.

days.headlineSummary: Today’s HN discussion was mostly mixed-neutral: Anthropic had the heaviest evidence volume with both praise and criticism [E1][E4][E6][E13], while OpenAI, Google Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot were in a close tie with limited-to-moderate evidence and a largely balanced or slightly negative tone [E2][E7][E8][E9][E14].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot had limited evidence and a neutral-to-slightly negative note, mainly because one commenter said work restrictions and backend lock-in made it unusable for their workflow [E14].

OpenAI drew mixed feedback with some praise for practical agent usefulness and easier rate limits, but also criticism over privacy, marketing, and AGI claims, leaving the result in a close tie with the other neutral providers [E8][E10][E11][E12][E13].

Google Gemini had limited evidence but remained mixed-neutral: one comment praised Gemini 3’s efficiency, while others criticized deprecation policy, stricter rate limits, and a quota failure [E7][E8][E9].

Anthropic saw the most discussion, with strong praise for Claude Code’s usefulness and task success offset by complaints about over-editing, higher pricing, and marketing similarities to peers, so the overall tone stayed mixed-neutral despite high evidence [E1][E4][E5][E6][E13].

evidence.excerpt: I'm either in a minority or a silent majority. Claude Code surpasses all my expectations. When it makes a mistake like over-editing, I explain the mistake, it fixes it, and I ask it to record what it learned in the relevant project-specific skills. It rarely makes that mistake again.

annotations.rationale: Strong praise for Claude Code's effectiveness and ability to improve after feedback.

evidence.excerpt: But then it decides to re-write the copy and layout of all pages. They were placeholders, but still? Codex also has a tendency to apply unwanted styles everywhere.

annotations.rationale: Criticizes Codex for unnecessary rewrites and unwanted styling changes.

evidence.excerpt: I've not seen over-editing in Claude Code or Codex in quite a while

annotations.rationale: Says Claude Code no longer shows a previously problematic behavior.
Says Codex no longer shows a previously problematic behavior.

evidence.excerpt: claude will happily refactor three unrelated files because it spotted a "pattern". we've ended up with a CLAUDE.md that's basically a list of "do not touch unless asked".

annotations.rationale: Complains that Claude makes unnecessary unrelated refactors and needs strict constraints.

evidence.excerpt: Opus and Sonnet in CC the same task successfully in a matter of minutes.

annotations.rationale: Reports Anthropic models succeeding quickly on a task where a local model failed.

evidence.excerpt: the token prices of these open source models are at a fraction of Anthropic's Opus 4.6

annotations.rationale: Frames Anthropic Opus as much more expensive than open-source alternatives.

evidence.excerpt: gemini 3 proved what is possible if you train a model for efficiency.

annotations.rationale: Credits Gemini 3 with demonstrating strong efficiency capabilities.

evidence.excerpt: It's interesting that, of the large inference providers, Google has one of the most inconvenient policies around model deprecation. They deprecate models exactly 1 year after releasing them and force you to move onto their next generation of models. I had assumed, because they are using their own silicon, that they would actually be able to offer better stability, but the opposite seems to be true. Their rate limiting is also much stricter than OpenAI for example.

OpenAI is used as the more favorable comparison on rate limiting.

annotations.rationale: In the ChatGPT workspace-agents story, the commenter reports strong practical usefulness despite some output limitations.

So super duper top secret they can’t even announce it. Because if their public models are any hint, you would never think we were 18 months away from human level machine intelligence.

days.headlineSummary: HN discussion is more negative toward Anthropic than OpenAI, with Anthropic criticized for being gated and untrustworthy while OpenAI gets some praise for openness despite quality complaints [E1][E2][E3].

ranking.summary: Anthropic drew the harsher sentiment, with comments calling its Mythos launch a “marketing stunt,” saying the team is no longer trusted, and contrasting Opus more favorably on hallucination rate [E1][E2][E3].
OpenAI was mixed to neutral overall: one comment praised it as the open option again, while another criticized GPT 5.5 for a huge hallucination rate [E1][E2].

evidence.excerpt: Everyone talked about the marketing stunt that was Anthropic's gated Mythos model with an 83% result on CyberGym. OpenAI just dropped GPT 5.5, which scores 82% and is open for anybody to use. I recommend anybody in offensive/defensive cybersecurity to experiment with this. This is the real data point we needed - without the hype! Never thought I'd say this but OpenAI is the 'open' option again.

The comment praises OpenAI for releasing GPT 5.5 as "open for anybody to use" and says OpenAI is the 'open' option again.

evidence.excerpt: Still huge hallucination rate, unfortunately at 86%. To compare, Opus sits at 36%.

annotations.rationale: In the GPT-5.5 story context, the comment says the model has a "huge hallucination rate," which is strong criticism of quality.
"Opus" is used as a favorable comparison point with a much lower hallucination rate, implying better quality.

evidence.excerpt: Damage is done for me though. Even just one of these things (messing with adaptive thinking) is enough for me to not trust them anymore. And then their A/B testing this week on pricing.

annotations.rationale: On the Claude Code postmortem story, the commenter says they do "not trust them anymore" and adds pricing criticism, showing strong negative sentiment toward Anthropic.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew the most negative discussion on cancellation, token issues, and poor support, while Google Gemini and OpenAI were viewed more positively in a close tie [E1][E2][E3].

ranking.summary: Anthropic was discussed negatively, with one comment saying Claude was not near the top models and a title citing token issues, declining quality, and poor support [E1][E2].

We will see if this changes the equation, but it feels like OpenAI is pretty far behind and playing catch up on all fronts. Though to be honest, "pretty far behind" is like 2-8 weeks in the AI world, so it may not matter a ton, it's mostly perception. And for me and my information bubble, perception of OpenAI is rock-bottom due to Sam Altman. From appearing unethical to appearing unhinged with demands from fabs and everything else, I'm not a fan.

evidence.excerpt: In my large enterprise world, AI adoption hasn't made it outside of the development teams - only developers have access to Github Copilot. Code takes 6-12 months to make it from commit to production. Development speed was never the bottleneck; it's all the other processes that take time: infra provisioning, testing, sign-offs, change management, deployment scheduling etc. AI makes these post-development bottlenecks worse. Changes are now piling up at the door waiting to get on a release train. Large enterprises need to learn how to ship software faster if they want to lock in ROI on their token spend. Unshipped code is a liability, not an asset.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly mentions GitHub Copilot and says the resulting AI-driven changes worsen downstream bottlenecks and create ROI problems.

evidence.excerpt: I am a physics professor and often use Gemini to check my papers. It is a formidable tool: it was able to find a clerical error (a missing imaginary unit in a complex mathematical expression) I was not able to find for days, and it often underlines connections between concepts and ideas that I overlooked. However, it often makes conceptual errors that I can spot only because I have good knowledge of the topic I am discussing. For instance, in 3D Clifford algebras it repeatedly confuses exponential of bivectors and of pseudoscalars. Good to know that ChatGPT 5.5 Pro can produce a publishable paper, but from what I have seen so far with Gemini, it seems to me that it is better to consider LLMs as very efficient students who can read papers and books in no time but still need a lot of mentoring.

annotations.rationale: The comment praises Gemini as 'a formidable tool' that found a real error, but also says it 'often makes conceptual errors' and still needs 'a lot of mentoring'.
The commenter says 'ChatGPT 5.5 Pro can produce a publishable paper,' which is mild praise for capability.
